pattern-printing.title: Pattern Printing
pattern-printing.summary: Use nested loops where the outer loop controls the row and the inner loop controls the column. Map row index to count of characters or spaces.
pattern-printing.whenToUse: Any problem that prints a 2D shape (stars, numbers, letters). Great warm-up for loops and index reasoning.
keyFormulas: Right triangle (row i has i stars)
Pyramid: leading spaces = n - i, stars = 2*i - 1
pitfalls: System.out.print vs println — only add newline after the inner loop.
Off-by-one: decide if i is 0-indexed or 1-indexed and stick with it.
pattern-printing.complexity: O(N²) for NxN grids, O(N²) for triangles.

digit-extraction.title: Digit Extraction (mod 10)
digit-extraction.summary: Pull digits off an integer by repeatedly taking n % 10 (last digit) and dividing n /= 10.
digit-extraction.whenToUse: Count digits, reverse number, digit sum, palindrome number, Armstrong, happy numbers.
pitfalls: n == 0 — the loop skips; special-case it if the problem considers 0 as having 1 digit.
Negatives — use Math.abs or sign handling.
digit-extraction.complexity: O(log₁₀ N) — one iteration per digit.

euclidean-gcd.title: Euclidean GCD
euclidean-gcd.summary: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Terminates when b == 0; at that point a is the gcd.
euclidean-gcd.whenToUse: GCD, LCM (lcm(a,b) = a*b / gcd(a,b)), reducing fractions, number theory problems.
pitfalls: gcd(0, x) = x — the algorithm handles this naturally.
Negative numbers — conventionally gcd is non-negative, so use Math.abs on inputs.
euclidean-gcd.complexity: O(log(min(a, b))) — way faster than brute O(min(a,b)).

recursion-basics.title: Recursion 101
recursion-basics.summary: A recursive function calls itself on a smaller sub-problem. Two ingredients: (1) a BASE CASE that stops the recursion, (2) a RECURSIVE CASE that reduces the problem.
recursion-basics.whenToUse: Problems naturally broken into sub-problems: factorial, Fibonacci, tree/graph traversal, divide-and-conquer.
mentalModel: Trust the recursion: assume fn(smaller) already works correctly.
Think of the call stack — each call frame holds its own local vars.
Convert to iteration if stack depth could blow up.
pitfalls: Missing/weak base case → infinite recursion → StackOverflowError.
Redundant recomputation (e.g., plain fib) → exponential time. Fix with memoization / DP.
recursion-basics.complexity: Depth = O(depth of recursion). Plain recursion without memoization can be exponential.

two-pointers.title: Two Pointers
two-pointers.summary: Two indices walk through the array — usually one from each end (for sorted arrays / palindromes) or both from the left at different speeds (write/scan, slow/fast).
two-pointers.whenToUse: Pairs with a target sum in sorted arrays, reverse in place, remove duplicates, partition / move zeros, palindrome checks, merge two sorted arrays.
pitfalls: Off-by-one with l < r vs l <= r.
Moving both pointers unconditionally — you usually advance only one per iteration.
two-pointers.complexity: O(N) with O(1) extra space — hard to beat.

sorting-comparison.title: Comparison Sorts (bubble / selection / insertion / merge / quick)
sorting-comparison.summary: Sorting by comparing pairs of elements. Different algorithms pick different orders and achieve different time/space/stability tradeoffs.
cheatsheet: Selection sort: find min of suffix, swap into place. O(N²) time, O(1) space. NOT stable.
Bubble sort: swap adjacent out-of-order pairs. O(N²) worst, O(N) best (sorted input) with early-exit. Stable.
Insertion sort: build sorted prefix by inserting next element. O(N²) worst, O(N) best. Stable. Great for small/nearly-sorted arrays.
Merge sort: divide & conquer. O(N log N) time, O(N) extra space. Stable.
Quick sort: partition around pivot, recurse. O(N log N) average, O(N²) worst, O(log N) space. NOT stable.
Best possible comparison sort: Ω(N log N) lower bound.
sorting-comparison.stabilityMatters: If you sort objects by a key, 'stable' preserves original order among equal keys. Useful for multi-level sorting.

divide-conquer.title: Divide and Conquer
divide-conquer.summary: Split the problem into smaller independent sub-problems, solve each recursively, combine the results.
divide-conquer.recurrence: T(N) = a * T(N/b) + O(f(N)) — solve with the Master Theorem.
classics: Merge sort — T(N) = 2T(N/2) + O(N) = O(N log N)
Binary search — T(N) = T(N/2) + O(1) = O(log N)
pitfalls: Avoid overlapping sub-problems — that's where DP beats D&C.

xor-trick.title: XOR Trick
xor-trick.summary: XOR has two superpowers: x ^ x == 0 and x ^ 0 == x. XORing a value twice cancels it out.
xor-trick.whenToUse: Find the unique element when others appear twice. Find missing number (XOR range ^ array). Swap two ints without a temp. Toggle bits.
niceProperties: Commutative and associative — order doesn't matter.
O(1) space — no hash set needed.

array-rotation.title: Array Rotation
array-rotation.summary: Rotate elements by K positions. The reverse trick rotates in O(N) time, O(1) space.
array-rotation.whyItWorks: After two reversals, each half's internal order is flipped; a final whole-array reverse un-flips and swaps the halves into position.
array-rotation.rightRotateByK: Same idea with step indices reversed, or equivalently left-rotate by (n - k).
pitfalls: Normalize k: k %= n before doing anything.

linear-scan.title: Single-Pass Linear Scan
linear-scan.summary: Walk the array once, maintaining a small amount of state (running max/min/count/sum). O(N) time, O(1) space — often optimal.
linear-scan.whenToUse: Find max/min/second-max, longest run, running sums, counting predicates, single-number XOR.
pitfalls: Initial value: use a[0] or Integer.MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE based on the problem.
Empty array — handle separately if the problem allows N == 0.

memoization.title: Memoization (top-down DP)
memoization.summary: Cache results of recursive calls so each unique input is computed once. Turns exponential recursion into polynomial.
memoization.whenToUse: Overlapping sub-problems: Fibonacci, grid paths, coin change, longest common subsequence.
memoization.bottomUp: After memoization works, convert to iterative DP: loop from base to target, fill a table.

reverse-trick.title: Reverse Trick
reverse-trick.summary: Three sub-array reversals rotate an array in-place with O(1) space. Also used in some string rearrangement problems.
reverse-trick.whyRemember: Comes up surprisingly often — array rotations, in-place shuffles, palindrome constructions.
